cards.text: What cardiovascular changes occur during exercise?
cards.answer: Heart rate increases (sympathetic), stroke volume increases (Starling mechanism), cardiac output increases up to 5-fold, systolic BP rises, diastolic BP stays same or falls, TPR decreases (muscle vasodilation), blood flow redistributed to muscles.

tip.tip: Think about what muscles need during exercise.

cards.text: How does the body acclimatise to high altitude?
cards.answer: Immediate: hyperventilation (low PO2 stimulates peripheral chemoreceptors), tachycardia. Days-weeks: increased EPO -> polycythaemia, increased 2,3-DPG (rightward shift of O2-Hb curve), increased capillary density, cellular adaptations.

tip.tip: The body needs more oxygen-carrying capacity.

cards.answer: Triggered by cold water on face: bradycardia, peripheral vasoconstriction, blood shift to core organs. Conserves oxygen. More pronounced in children. Protective mechanism that explains survival in cold water drowning cases.

Thermoregulatory failure: core temperature exceeds 40 degrees C, sweating ceases, CNS dysfunction (confusion, seizures, coma). Medical emergency. Unlike heat exhaustion (intact sweating, lower temperature), heat stroke requires immediate cooling.

tip.tip: Sweating stops - this is the danger sign.

cards.text: What metabolic changes occur in starvation?
cards.answer: Phase 1 (hours): glycogen depletion. Phase 2 (days): gluconeogenesis from amino acids, lipolysis, ketogenesis. Phase 3 (weeks): brain adapts to ketone bodies, protein sparing. Basal metabolic rate decreases. Eventually muscle wasting occurs.

tip.tip: The body progressively shifts fuel sources.

cards.text: What is the stress response (fight or flight)?
cards.answer: Sympathoadrenal activation: adrenaline and noradrenaline release. Effects: tachycardia, bronchodilation, pupil dilation, glycogenolysis, blood flow to muscles, reduced GI activity. Cortisol (HPA axis) provides sustained metabolic support.

cards.text: How does ageing affect physiological function?
cards.answer: Reduced cardiac reserve, decreased lung elasticity and vital capacity, reduced GFR, decreased bone density, reduced muscle mass (sarcopenia), impaired glucose tolerance, decreased immune function, reduced sensory acuity.

tip.tip: Gradual decline in reserve capacity of all systems.

cards.text: What is the physiology of sleep?
cards.answer: Controlled by suprachiasmatic nucleus (circadian clock) and sleep-promoting neurons (VLPO, adenosine). NREM: parasympathetic dominance, growth hormone release. REM: irregular autonomic activity, muscle atonia, dreaming. Sleep deprivation impairs cognition and immunity.

tip.tip: Two interacting drives: circadian and homeostatic.
